column4: Systematic comparison of therapeutic dose levels with observed benefit and toxicity to guide safer treatment.
Study and synthesis of carbon compounds, enabling systematic design and production of many modern medicines and dyes.
Chemical extraction and purification of active plant alkaloids such as morphine and quinine into measurable drug substances.
Mechanical compression of powders into standardized oral dosage forms for reproducible dosing and mass distribution.
Aseptic preparation and sealed injectable medicines that require sterilization, containers, and controlled clinical dosing.
Clinical experiment design that assigns participants to treatment or control groups by chance to estimate intervention effects.
Clinical trial design in which participants and investigators are masked to treatment assignment to reduce bias.
Quality systems for pharmaceutical manufacturing that control facilities, processes, documentation, testing, and batch release.
Laboratory methods that measure whether pathogens are inhibited by specific antibiotics to guide treatment and resistance monitoring.
Quantitative study of how drug concentration, absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and effect relate over time.
Dosage technologies that release active ingredients over time using coatings, matrices, polymers, or osmotic systems.
Design, synthesis, and optimization of drug-like molecules by combining organic chemistry, biology, and pharmacology.
Automated testing of large compound libraries against biological targets using robotics, assays, and data systems.
Parallel synthesis of large chemical libraries for screening and optimization of drug-like compounds.
Iterative improvement of candidate molecules for potency, selectivity, exposure, safety, and manufacturability before clinical development.
Drug design approach using three-dimensional molecular structures to guide binding, optimization, and selectivity.
Computational modeling, docking, simulation, and informatics used to prioritize and optimize candidate medicines.
Cell-based and biochemical assays for absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity before human trials.
Standardized preclinical safety studies with controlled protocols, records, and quality systems for regulatory submission.
Use of genetic variation to predict drug response, dosing, efficacy, or adverse reactions.
Post-market systems for collecting, analyzing, and acting on reports of suspected drug side effects and safety signals.
Sequential human drug trials assessing safety, dosing, efficacy, and comparative benefit before regulatory approval.
Formal review of evidence on quality, safety, and efficacy before medicines are authorized for clinical use.
Therapeutic proteins produced by engineered cells, including recombinant hormones, enzymes, cytokines, and clotting factors.
Controlled production, purification, testing, and release of biologic medicines from living cells or biological systems.
Viral and nonviral delivery systems engineered to introduce therapeutic genetic material into patient cells or tissues.
Monoclonal-antibody cancer therapies that block inhibitory immune checkpoints such as PD-1, PD-L1, or CTLA-4.
Highly similar follow-on biologic medicines supported by analytical, manufacturing, clinical, and regulatory comparability evidence.
Nanoscale carriers such as liposomes, polymer particles, and lipid nanoparticles used to protect, target, or control medicines in the body.
Integrated production systems that manufacture drug substance or drug product through continuously controlled processing rather than discrete batches.
Use of routinely collected health data to study medicine safety, effectiveness, utilization, and regulatory questions after or alongside trials.
Forecast drug-discovery systems that combine generative models, robotic synthesis, automated assays, and active learning to optimize candidates.
